exemplar.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

detailedExemplarData.title: To what extent do you agree with the claim "all models are wrong, but some are useful" (attributed to George Box)? Discuss with reference to mathematics and one other area of knowledge.

strengths: Sustained, focused engagement with the prescribed title throughout the discussion
Clear, coherent arguments effectively linked to Boxâs aphorism and to the chosen areas of knowledge
Well-chosen real-world examples (e.g. Kant vs Constant, Murrayâs Bell Curve, Euclid/Hilbert/Tarski, Bayesian statistics) that underpin the analysis
Distinct treatment of Mathematics and the Human Sciences, showing appreciation of their differing epistemic aims
Recognition and some evaluation of alternative perspectives, particularly ethical objections in the human sciences and the objectivityâsubjectivity tension in mathematics
weaknesses: Evaluation of alternative points of view is asserted rather than deeply critiqued or exemplified
Implications of arguments for knowledge production and real-world decision-making are noted but not explored in sustained depth
Some passages lapse into description or narrative summary before analytical re-engagement
Several key claims (e.g. subjectivity improving predictive accuracy, proof-based certainty) are oversimplified or lack necessary caveats
The thesis and conclusion restate Boxâs claim without sufficiently qualifying or indicating future directions for inquiry
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay clearly surpasses the 5â6 band by maintaining a sustained focus on the title, offering coherent arguments, and providing well-chosen examples that link effectively to the areas of knowledge. There is an awareness of alternative perspectives and a reasoned comparison of the usefulness and accuracy of models in each discipline. However, the critical evaluation of these perspectives and the systematic exploration of the broader implications fall short of the consistently incisive, deeply evaluated discussion expected for a 9â10 band. The combination of strong focus and illustrative examples with only partial development of evaluative depth justifies a score of 7, in line with the 7â8 descriptor.

annotations.comment: The argument that subjectivity improves predictive accuracy needs balance by acknowledging scenarios where subjectivity introduces error; address potential downsides to strengthen the case.

You argue that proofs ââallow for models to become more certain over time,ââ but this simplification ignores GÃ¶delâs incompleteness theorems; consider mentioning limits of proof-based certainty.
The assertion that human-science models are âsubjectiveâ is unsupported; include a specific example of subjectivity impacting model formation in the human sciences to ground this claim.

The Kant vs Constant example is well chosen to illustrate contrasting ethical models in the human sciences; deepen the evaluation by examining the implications of deontologyâs absoluteness for knowledge evolution.

The thesis restates Boxâs claim without explicitly stating the extent of agreement or outlining how mathematics and the human sciences will be compared. Consider refining the thesis to assert a clear position and preview key arguments.
The description of mathematical models remains at a high level; explicate how abstraction and proof generate mathematical knowledge to strengthen the discussion beyond objectivity.
Agreement with Boxâs claim is asserted without any supporting example here; introduce a real-life situation in the introduction to justify your stance and set up later analysis.

This topic sentence claims ethical repercussions limit inaccurate models but lacks definition of what makes a model ethically harmful; clarify the criteria for âethical repercussionsâ to strengthen your argument.
The analysis of Kantian ethics focuses on narrative but doesnât evaluate how contradictions drive new knowledge; explain how this debate advances our understanding of model utility.
The Bell Curve example is introduced narratively but missing critique of its methodological assumptions; analyze how measurement bias and sampling affect its epistemic validity.
Noting that experts disregarded Murrayâs model is a strong real-world link; consider assessing how authority and academic consensus affect the perceived usefulness of models.
When stating that human-science models become inapplicable due to ethics, reference a theory of ethics or epistemology to underpin your claim and strengthen its justification.
The description of why the Bell Curve was rejected focuses on politics but lacks discussion of its statistical methodology; critique its use of correlation versus causation.
The summary conflates philosophical and political examples without clearly distinguishing their epistemic implications; separate the effects on model usefulness in each context.
This paragraph concludes without explicitly linking back to Boxâs aphorism; make a direct connection to âall models are wrong, but some are usefulâ to maintain thematic coherence.

The claim that Hilbertâs axiomatic improvements showcase proof methodology is asserted without exploring why second-order axioms enhance model certainty; clarify how this process refines mathematical knowledge.
Your explanation of Hilbertâs and Tarskiâs improvements effectively demonstrates model refinement through proof, reinforcing the argument on how mathematical methodology drives objectivity.
You note real-world applications of Euclidean geometry but donât provide concrete examples; include a case study (e.g., architectural design or physics) to illustrate the utility of these axioms.
The transition to Bayesian statistics lacks a clear definition of frequentist versus Bayesian paradigms; explicitly contrast their foundational philosophies to highlight model subjectivity.
When describing Bayesian subjectivity, the historical origin of Bayesâ theorem would contextualize its epistemic significance; consider adding a brief overview of its probabilistic foundations.
The conclusion asserts that all models produce new knowledge but overlooks situations where flawed models mislead researchers; include a caveat to acknowledge these counterexamples.
The final summary restates earlier points but does not evaluate the broader implications for knowledge production across communities; reflect on how model utility varies by audience and purpose.
Your essay ends without suggesting future directions for exploring model usefulness; propose an area for further investigation to demonstrate critical engagement with the topicâs implications.
The concluding sentence ââBoxâs claim holds true in both AOKsââ is too absolute; qualify this assertion by acknowledging remaining uncertainties or counterclaims.
The conclusion synthesis is descriptive rather than evaluative; deepen the analysis by discussing the long-term impact of relying on imperfect models in real-world decision-making.

described by levels
1â5 or is not a

on the list for the

The discussion is weakly connected to the title. While there may be links to the areas of knowledge, any relevant points are descriptive or consist only of unsupported assertions.

The discussion is connected to the title and makes superficial or limited links to areas of knowledge. The discussion is largely descriptive. Limited arguments are offered but they are unclear and are not supported by effective examples.

The discussion
is focused on
the title and is
developed with
some links to
areas of

Arguments are

examples.
There is some

different points
of view.

The discussion is focused on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and supported by examples. There is awareness and some evaluation of different points of view.

The discussion has a sustained focus on the title and is linked effectively to areas of knowledge. Arguments are clear, coherent and effectively supported by specific examples. The implications of arguments are considered. There is clear awareness and evaluation of different points of view.
